metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC #3's oracle to enumerate the specific Data-contract fields the end-to-end test must assert are rendered (e.g., `scenarios`, `impact`, `suggested_fix`, `verification`, `validity`, `reason`, `move_id`/`finding_refs` from `coaching[]`), or add a dedicated AC for field-level rendering coverage.
evidence: Data contract table: 'Required fields: finding, criteria, location, evidence, scenarios, impact, suggested_fix, verification, validity, priority, decision, reason, block_threshold, blocking_enabled' and 'each round's coaching[] ({move_id, finding_refs})'

AC #3 oracle: 'asserts on the rendered response' covering only 'pass-1..4 detail, per-finding resolved thresholds, and the completion criteria[] rows' — fields like scenarios, impact, suggested_fix, verification, validity, reason, move_id, finding_refs are NOT listed in the oracle
findings.finding: The Data contract section enumerates a detailed set of required rendered fields per UI region (`scenarios`, `impact`, `suggested_fix`, `verification`, `validity`, `reason`, `move_id`, `finding_refs`, `block_threshold`, `blocking_enabled`, plus the full `coaching[]` shape), but AC #3's oracle only checks at a coarse level ('pass-1..4 detail, per-finding resolved thresholds, and the completion criteria[] rows'). The detailed field list is described deliverable work — the UI must surface these fields — yet no AC verifies them, leaving a large gap between the Data contract specification and what the acceptance test will actually assert.
findings.location: Data contract table vs. Acceptance Criteria AC #3
scenarios: An implementer renders only a subset of the Data contract fields (e.g., omits `scenarios`, `suggested_fix`, `verification`) and all four ACs still pass — the audit UI is incomplete but undetected by the test suite.
A code reviewer checking AC #3 has no way to determine which individual fields the test must assert on.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or bullet list to AC #3's oracle: 'The test must assert that for at least one finding, the rendered page includes: `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `suggested_fix`, `verification`, `validity`, `priority`, `decision`, `reason`, `block_threshold`; and for at least one coaching entry: `move_id` and `finding_refs`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the 'rebar audit serve' AC oracle to name the specific rendered elements it must assert (e.g., presence of pass-1..4 sections, at least one per-finding `block_threshold` value, and at least one `criteria[]` row) so the test cannot be satisfied by a bare HTTP-200 check.
evidence: AC says 'asserts on the rendered response' without naming which fields/elements are asserted

Success Criteria names 'four passes shown including the per-finding resolved thresholds and the completion criteria[] rows' as the content goal, but this is not reflected as an explicit assertion requirement in the oracle
By contrast, the navigation AC names the exact content invariant: 'asserting each renders its (distinct from latest) content'
findings.finding: The oracle for the main 'rebar audit serve' AC under-specifies what the e2e test must assert in the rendered response, creating a loophole where an implementer could satisfy the AC with a trivial HTTP-200 check while omitting verification of the four-pass breakdown, per-finding resolved thresholds, and completion criteria[] rows.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet: 'Oracle: a live end-to-end test that starts the server and asserts on the rendered response.'
scenarios: An implementer writes an e2e test that GETs the page and asserts HTTP 200 — the AC passes but the four-pass UI rendering is never verified.
A reviewer approving the story cannot tell from the AC whether pass-level detail was tested.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'asserts on the rendered response' with: 'asserts on the rendered response, verifying: (a) all four pass sections (pass-1 findings, pass-2 verification, pass-3 determination, pass-4 coaching) are present in the HTML; (b) at least one finding's resolved `block_threshold` value is rendered; (c) at least one completion `criteria[]` row with its `kind` is rendered.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the Data Contract's `coaching[]` row to list all fields Epic 1 persists per coaching note: `{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching}` (the rendered prose), matching the actual sidecar schema in story a3db.
evidence: Plan table row: `full retained per-round sidecars (newest-first) + each round's coaching[] ({move_id, finding_refs})`
sidecar.py lines 443-454: persists {move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching} with full rendered prose
test_audit_coaching_fidelity.py: asserts move_name, subject, and rendered coaching prose are all present in the sidecar payload
findings.finding: The Data Contract table's `coaching[]` row lists only `{move_id, finding_refs}` as the required fields for 'Round history + convergence', but Epic 1's sidecar (plan_review/sidecar.py lines 443-454) persists `{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching}` (full rendered prose). An executor building the UI interface from the table definition alone would omit `move_name`, `subject`, and the rendered `coaching` prose — making it impossible to satisfy the AC requirement to show 'each review's four passes' (pass-4 coaching detail).
findings.location: Data contract table — 'Round history + convergence' row; also Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria ('four passes shown')
scenarios: An implementer reads the data contract table and defines their UI data model from it, building a coaching panel that shows only move IDs and finding references — with no rendered prose — and the AC 'four passes shown' is not met.
Epic 1 story A ships `coaching` prose in the sidecar; Epic 2 ignores it because the interface contract only listed `{move_id, finding_refs}`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `{move_id, finding_refs}` in the coaching[] row with `{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs[], coaching}` — the full shape already committed to sidecar.py and verified by test_audit_coaching_fidelity.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen ff6f's round-navigation oracle to explicitly require seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds and asserting the older round of EACH renders distinct content, matching parent PAC-4 exactly.
evidence: Parent PAC-4 oracle: 'a live end-to-end test seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds on one ticket, selecting the older round in each gate, and asserting each renders its (distinct from latest) content'

ff6f AC oracle: 'an E2E test seeding ≥2 rounds asserting the selector shows N, the default is the latest, and requesting the older round renders its (distinct-from-latest) findings' — omits the requirement to test BOTH gate types independently
ff6f AC text does say 'the retained plan- and code-review rounds' in the criterion prose but the oracle does not enforce ≥2 of each
container pairing: parent + a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c/ff6f-b655-4058-4308
findings.finding: Child ff6f's round-navigation acceptance criterion oracle only mandates 'seeding ≥2 rounds' generically, while the parent PAC-4 explicitly requires ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds, with an older round of EACH gate independently selectable and rendering distinct content. ff6f's oracle could be satisfied by testing only one gate type, leaving the other gate's history navigation unverified.
findings.location: child ff6f-b655-4058-4308, Acceptance Criteria: '◂ round n of N ▸ selector' bullet
scenarios: An implementation that seeds 2 plan-review rounds and 0 code-review rounds passes ff6f's oracle but fails PAC-4
A code-review round-navigation bug goes undetected because the E2E only exercises plan-review history
The parent's completion verifier checks PAC-4 against ff6f's oracle and finds the code-review half uncovered
findings.suggested_fix: Revise ff6f's round-selector AC oracle to read: 'an E2E test seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds on one ticket, asserting the selector for each gate shows N, defaults to latest, and requesting the older round of each gate renders its distinct findings — consistent with the parent epic's PAC-4 oracle.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback rendering behavior for v1 sidecar records in the history (e.g., show available fields and display a 'pre-enrichment round — provenance fields unavailable' notice for missing `evidence`/`scenarios`/`block_threshold`).
evidence: Plan: 'This epic MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed'
test_sidecar_reader.py line 171: '# A genuine v1 record: no evidence/scenarios/block_threshold/blocking_enabled on the finding.'
test_sidecar_reader.py line 190: 'assert "block_threshold" not in got_v1["findings"][0]'
Acceptance Criteria: 'an OLDER round (not just the latest) of EACH can be selected and its distinct content renders'
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback rendering behavior when a sidecar in the retained history is a v1 record (lacking `evidence`, `scenarios`, `block_threshold`, `blocking_enabled`). The codebase confirms v1 sidecars exist in the wild (test_sidecar_reader.py lines 171-190 explicitly tests a v1 record without these fields). The plan says 'MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed' — but the retained history the UI navigates will always contain pre-enrichment (v1) rounds for any ticket reviewed before Epic 1 lands.
findings.location: Data contract section — 'Finding-provenance (per finding)' and 'Threshold meter (per finding)' rows; also Acceptance Criteria ('navigable across the retained review history')
scenarios: A user navigates to an older review round; the sidecar is v1 — the UI crashes or renders a blank panel because the expected fields are absent, with no specified degraded state.
The 'MUST NOT render' guard is interpreted as 'suppress the whole history tab' for any ticket with v1 history entries, making round navigation impossible for legacy tickets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default port for `rebar audit serve` in the plan (e.g., 'binds to 127.0.0.1:\<port\> — default 8421') so the executor and test oracle have a single reference.
evidence: Plan says: 'A `rebar audit serve` subcommand starts a read-only web server' with no port number stated anywhere in the document.
ABSENCE: no default port (e.g., 8421, 8080) is mentioned in Scope, Compatibility, or Acceptance Criteria.
findings.finding: The `rebar audit serve` subcommand's default port is never specified, leaving an executor to choose arbitrarily — a defaultable gap that should be stated in the plan.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — `rebar audit serve` subcommand description
scenarios: An executor implementing the CLI could pick any port, creating inconsistency between stories, tests, and documentation.
The end-to-end test oracle ('starts the server and asserts on the rendered response') needs a known port to issue the HTTP request.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or Success Criteria: '`rebar audit serve` binds to `127.0.0.1:<port>` (default: 8421, overridable via `--port` or `[ui] port` config key).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pass-to-field mapping (e.g., a small table or prose paragraph) stating which data-contract fields or field groups correspond to each of the four displayed passes (pass-1 findings, pass-2 verification, pass-3 determination, pass-4 coaching) so template authors have an unambiguous layout contract.
evidence: AC states: 'plan/completion/code panels with pass-1..4 detail'

Data contract table maps fields (`finding`, `criteria`, `verification`, `validity`, `decision`, `reason`, `block_threshold`, etc.) to UI *regions* (Finding-provenance, Round history, Threshold meter, Completion panel) but NOT to pass-1/2/3/4 tabs or sections.
The `coaching[]` field (`{move_id, finding_refs}`) is listed under 'Round history + convergence' without stating it is the pass-4 source; the mapping is implied but not declared.
findings.finding: The plan promises 'each review's four passes shown' but the data-contract table maps per-finding fields to UI regions without specifying which fields constitute each structural pass (pass-1 findings, pass-2 verification, pass-3 determination, pass-4 coaching), leaving a template author uncertain about how to organize the display.
findings.location: Data contract table / Acceptance Criteria — 'four passes shown including the per-finding resolved thresholds'
scenarios: A Jinja2 template author doesn't know whether `verification`+`validity` is pass-2, whether `decision`+`reason` is pass-3, or whether all fields are shown flat in a single per-finding block.
If child stories interpret the pass structure differently, the rendered UI may not match the AC oracle assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md or ADR entry capturing the 'optional lazy-imported FastAPI extra' pattern (loopback-only, no client build step, never imported by core) as the rebar convention for optional web surfaces, so future contributors can follow it consistently.
evidence: 'the reviewbot FastAPI app is the established "thin, lazily-imported, never-required-by-core" precedent'
No mention of an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry anywhere in the plan
The plan explicitly frames this as codifying a pattern for future optional features, raising the stakes for documentation
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — the 'optional, lazy-imported, loopback-only FastAPI sub-app as a rebar extra' pattern — and names it as the precedent for future optional features, but does not call for capturing it in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc update.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Compatibility & rollback section
scenarios: A future developer adds a second optional feature extra and re-invents or violates the lazy-import contract because no canonical doc exists
A reviewer auditing the repo cannot find the rationale for the loopback-only / no-auth design choice
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or a section in CLAUDE.md) titled something like 'Optional web surface pattern' that documents: (1) extras must be lazy-imported via a guard, (2) the server binds loopback-only, (3) no client build step, (4) the `[section] enabled = false` default-off convention. Reference it from both the reviewbot extra and this new UI extra.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the full `nava-rebar[ui]` extra dependency set in the plan (at minimum `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `jinja2`), since FastAPI does not bundle jinja2 and the existing `reviewbot` extra omits it.
evidence: Plan: 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack'
Plan: 'its web deps live in a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra'
pyproject.toml reviewbot extra: `reviewbot = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — no jinja2

fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates EXISTS in installed env (confirmed via resolve_symbol) but only because jinja2 is separately installed — FastAPI's pyproject declares jinja2 as an optional dep, not a required one
Plan never lists the concrete pip deps for the `[ui]` extra
findings.finding: The new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra's dependency set is never enumerated in the plan, and `jinja2` is NOT bundled with `fastapi` — it is a separate optional dep. The existing `reviewbot` extra (the named reuse target) only declares `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, `boto3`. A `rebar audit serve` that uses `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` would raise `ImportError` at runtime unless `jinja2` is explicitly added to the `[ui]` extra.
findings.location: Approach rationale / Compatibility & rollback sections; implied `nava-rebar[ui]` extra
scenarios: A user installs `nava-rebar[ui]` as defined by the plan (which only mentions reusing the reviewbot stack), enables the flag, and runs `rebar audit serve` — the Jinja2Templates import fails with ImportError because jinja2 was not declared in the extra.
CI installs only the `[ui]` extra without jinja2; the serve subcommand crashes on first template render.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `jinja2>=3` to the `[ui]` extra declaration: `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2>=3"]`. Alternatively, if the UI extra intentionally reuses the `reviewbot` extra, update `reviewbot` to include `jinja2>=3` and have `[ui]` depend on it (e.g. `ui = ["nava-rebar[reviewbot]", "jinja2>=3"]`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior when the `[ui]` extra IS installed but `[ui] enabled = false` (e.g. '`rebar audit serve` exits with a clear "UI feature is disabled; set `[ui] enabled = true` in rebar.toml" message, without importing any web dep').
evidence: Plan: 'The whole feature is default-off (`[ui] enabled = false`) and its web deps live in a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra, lazy-imported — so it is inert when disabled and when the extra is absent.'

Plan AC4: 'With the feature disabled (default), no web dependency is imported and all existing commands behave unchanged.' — only specifies the default/disabled case, not the extra-installed-but-disabled case.
ABSENCE: no plan statement about behavior when `nava-rebar[ui]` IS installed but `[ui] enabled` remains false
findings.finding: The plan's default-off mechanism specifies two independent gates (config `[ui] enabled = false` AND extra not installed), but leaves the 'extra installed, flag still false' path unspecified. A user who installs `nava-rebar[ui]` but has not yet set `[ui] enabled = true` will hit an undefined behavior: does `rebar audit serve` fail with a clear 'feature disabled' error, silently succeed, or is the config flag the sole runtime gate once the extra is present?
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section; Acceptance Criteria bullet 4
scenarios: Developer installs `pip install nava-rebar[ui]` to explore the feature but hasn't edited rebar.toml; `rebar audit serve` behavior is undefined — does it start anyway (flag ignored), refuse with a legible error, or behave as if the extra is absent?

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence in the Approach rationale explaining why FastAPI+Jinja2 is preferred over the stdlib `http.server` pattern already used by `editor_server.py` for a read-only loopback single-user local UI (the closest in-repo prior art for this use case).
evidence: Plan states: 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack'
src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py uses stdlib http.server for a read-only loopback single-user local UI — the same scenario
The workflow editor is the only existing in-repo local web UI precedent; the reviewbot is a persistent webhook receiver, not a local audit tool
findings.finding: The plan claims to be 'REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack' but the closest in-repo prior art for a read-only, loopback, single-user local web UI is the workflow editor_server.py, which uses stdlib http.server (not FastAPI+Jinja2). The plan does not discuss this established pattern or justify why FastAPI+Jinja2 is preferred over it for an identical use case (loopback, single-user, no client build, read-only).
scenarios: A reviewer or implementor may reasonably ask why the heavier FastAPI+Jinja2 stack is needed when the in-repo pattern for this exact scenario is stdlib http.server, and may push back or choose the wrong approach without explicit justification.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach rationale: 'The workflow editor uses stdlib http.server for its loopback UI; FastAPI+Jinja2 is chosen here instead because [specific reason, e.g., async support for future extensibility / richer routing / Jinja2 template inheritance vs raw HTML string generation in editor_server.py].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the `nava-rebar[ui]` extra's explicit dep list in the plan (at minimum `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, and `jinja2`) to verify jinja2 is not assumed to arrive transitively from the reviewbot extra.
evidence: pyproject.toml line 93: `reviewbot = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — no jinja2
Plan states: 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack'
FastAPI docs: Jinja2Templates requires `pip install jinja2` (or `fastapi[standard]`) separately from bare `fastapi`
The plan's new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra dep list is never enumerated
findings.finding: The plan asserts Jinja2 templating is available via 'reusing the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack,' but the reviewbot extra declares only `fastapi` and `uvicorn[standard]` — not `jinja2` explicitly. FastAPI's `Jinja2Templates` requires jinja2 as a separate install; it is not bundled with `fastapi` alone. The plan never specifies the `[ui]` extra's dep list, leaving the jinja2 dependency unverified.
findings.location: Approach rationale / Compatibility & rollback / Acceptance Criteria (the '[ui] extra' references throughout)
scenarios: An implementor who installs only the existing reviewbot extra finds Jinja2Templates raises ImportError at runtime because jinja2 is absent.
The [ui] extra is specified without jinja2, causing the server-rendered page to fail at template load time.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Compatibility & rollback or Scope section, add: 'The `nava-rebar[ui]` extra declares: `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, `jinja2`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare `jinja2` (and any other Jinja2Templates prerequisites such as `python-multipart`) in the new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra definition in `pyproject.toml`, rather than relying on it as an undeclared transitive of bare `fastapi`.
evidence: Plan states: 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the reviewbot extra's already-shipped web stack'
pyproject.toml reviewbot extra: `reviewbot = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — no jinja2
jinja2 is absent from ALL entries in pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies]
`fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` resolves in the current venv only because jinja2 happens to be transitively installed — a bare `fastapi` install does not guarantee this
Evidence class: Partially-verified — Jinja2Templates surface exists in FastAPI, but its availability is NOT guaranteed by the bare `fastapi` package declaration the plan relies on
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'FastAPI + Jinja2' server-rendered templates, but `jinja2` is not declared in `pyproject.toml` — not in the existing `reviewbot` extra nor anywhere else. Bare `fastapi` (as currently declared: `reviewbot = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]`) does NOT guarantee jinja2 is installed; `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` raises `ImportError` at runtime without it. The new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra definition must explicitly list `jinja2` (and likely `python-multipart` if form/query handling is needed) to make the Jinja2Templates integration reliable.
findings.location: Approach rationale section — 'Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2'; Compatibility & rollback section — 'its web deps live in a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra'
scenarios: A fresh `pip install 'nava-rebar[ui]'` that resolves `fastapi` without jinja2 as a transitive would cause `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` to raise ImportError at server startup, silently breaking the entire render path.
A future FastAPI release that further optionalizes its template dependency could break an existing install.
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyproject.toml`, define the new extra as `ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]` (mirroring the `reviewbot` extra's pattern but adding `jinja2` explicitly, since the `reviewbot` extra doesn't need templates and omits it).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and assign ownership of the 'story E read layer': either add a missing child story to this epic that builds the data-access layer, or explicitly document that the read layer is part of a3d7's scope and add its spec to a3d7's acceptance criteria.
evidence: a3d7 scope: 'one index route using the read layer from story E'
ff6f scope: 'add the /ticket/\u003cid\u003e route consuming the story-E read layer'
Sibling roster contains only a3d7 and ff6f — no story E is present
Parent epic scope says OUT: 'any data-model/sidecar change (owned by the dependency epic)' but does not explicitly OUT a read-layer abstraction within this epic
container pairing: parent + a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c/ff6f-b655-4058-4308
findings.finding: Both child stories reference 'the story-E read layer' / 'read layer from story E' as a concrete dependency, but no story E appears in the sibling roster (only a3d7 and ff6f). The read layer that mediates between Epic 1's enriched sidecar data and the server routes is a precondition for BOTH the index page (a3d7) and the per-ticket page (ff6f), yet no child in this epic owns building or specifying it.
findings.location: child a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c (server.py scope: 'one index route using the read layer from story E'); child ff6f-b655-4058-4308 (scope: 'add the /ticket/<id> route consuming the story-E read layer')
scenarios: At implementation time, neither story has an authoritative spec for the read layer's interface (method signatures, return shapes, error handling for missing/partial data), causing integration ambiguity or duplicated ad-hoc implementations in each story
The read layer for the index (listing audited tickets) may require different queries than the per-ticket read layer, and without an owning story the two could diverge
If 'story E' was a dropped/renamed child, its scope (read layer construction, data access patterns, error states for missing Epic 1 fields) is now unowned and neither child will implement it
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope bullet to a3d7 (or create a new child story) explicitly owning the read layer: its interface (e.g., `get_audited_tickets() -> list[TicketSummary]`, `get_ticket_audit(id) -> TicketAudit`), its data sources (Epic 1 sidecar paths), and its error contract (missing fields, absent gate). Reference that interface by name in ff6f so both stories bind to the same contract.

evidence: Session log child story ff6f-b655-4058-4308 is described as 'audit per-ticket page (finding-centric IA, gate strip, neutral threshold meter, ...'
The plan's Scope IN section says only 'the optional web-server surface ... and the per-ticket audit page rendering' with no mention of a gate strip component.
The plan's Acceptance Criteria describe 'plan/completion/code panels with pass-1..4 detail, per-finding resolved thresholds, and the completion criteria[] rows' — no gate strip is named.
No occurrence of 'gate strip' anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: The plan silently drops the 'gate strip' UI component that the session log names as a distinct element of the per-ticket page.
scenarios: The implementing story ff6f is built without a gate strip because the epic plan never required it, and the per-ticket page lacks the visual component the session log intended for gate-level summary navigation.
Auditors cannot quickly locate which gate (plan review vs. completion vs. code review) a finding belongs to because the gate-strip navigation rail was never specified.

evidence: Session log child story ff6f-b655-4058-4308 names the component 'neutral threshold meter'.
The plan's data contract table names the same component 'Threshold meter (per finding)' — the word 'neutral' is absent.
The plan's Acceptance Criteria refer to 'per-finding resolved thresholds' without any neutrality/presentation constraint.
No occurrence of 'neutral' in the plan to describe the meter's visual treatment or bias.
findings.finding: The plan silently drops the 'neutral' design constraint on the threshold meter, narrowing the session log's 'neutral threshold meter' to an unqualified 'threshold meter'.
scenarios: The implementing story renders a threshold meter styled in a pass/fail (red/green) manner rather than the neutral presentation the session log intended, contradicting the design decision without documented rationale.
The 'neutral' qualifier likely encodes an explicit design decision (avoid alarming coloration for a read-only audit view) that is lost, causing re-work during implementation review.

evidence: Session log child story ff6f-b655-4058-4308 leads with 'finding-centric IA' as the first named attribute of the per-ticket page.

The plan's Acceptance Criteria describe the page as showing 'ticket details and the plan/completion/code panels with pass-1..4 detail' — a panel-centric framing, not an explicit finding-centric IA statement.
The plan's Approach rationale discusses FastAPI/Jinja2 server rendering but says nothing about findings being the primary navigational or organizational unit of the page.
The data contract table is organized by consumer region (panels), not by findings as the primary IA axis.
findings.finding: The plan does not state 'finding-centric IA' as an explicit information-architecture requirement, silently narrowing the named IA design decision expressed in the session log child story.
scenarios: The implementing story organizes the page around gate panels (plan panel, completion panel, code panel) with findings as subordinate items inside each panel, rather than findings being the top-level navigable entity the session log intended.
Without an explicit 'finding-centric IA' requirement, reviewers cannot verify at acceptance whether the IA matches the design intent.

findings.finding: Both children reference a 'story E read layer' as the runtime data-access interface consumed by the server routes (child A: 'one index route using the read layer from story E'; child B scope: 'add the /ticket/<id> route consuming the story-E read layer'), but story E appears in neither the sibling roster nor either child's own scope as a deliverable. No sibling owns the delivery or specification of this read layer within the epic, creating a scope-owned-by-none gap.
findings.location: child a3d7-fadb-e7e9-4e2c (Scope / What) and child ff6f-b655-4058-4308 (Scope)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a third child story to this epic that owns the read-layer module (data access abstraction over Epic 1's sidecar files), with both A and B depending on it; or (b) if story E is an Epic 1 deliverable, name it explicitly in the parent's data contract table and add an import/interface stub to child A's scope so both children have a concrete contract to code against.

findings.finding: Child a3d7 registers the `audit` CLI intercept as dispatching both `serve` AND `show` subcommands, but neither child defines, scopes, or specifies AC for the `show` subcommand. It is referenced but owned by no sibling.
findings.suggested_fix: If `show` is intentionally deferred, replace the dispatch with a 'not yet implemented' SystemExit (matching the disabled-path pattern) and document it as a future story. If it is in scope, add its specification to the appropriate child.

findings.finding: The data contract table requires `evidence` and `scenarios` as fields for the 'Finding-provenance' UI region (sourced from plan-review v2 story A / code-review v2 story B), but the current plan-review `_slim` explicitly excludes them with the comment 'deliberately exclude runtime-only carriers (e.g. scenarios, evidence)'. Epic 1 story A must override this exclusion decision, but the plan gives no specification of how (add to `_slim`, a separate sidecar section, a second payload key) — leaving story A's scope ambiguous.
findings.location: Data contract table — 'Finding-provenance (per finding)' row; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py _slim comment (line 313)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
